summary: Gruber pushes back against tech media hype over Microsoft's announced Zune Wi-Fi sharing feature, pointing out that the Zune is still vaporware and the feature isn't even finished being designed. He quotes a TechCrunch writer breathlessly asking what Apple will do in response, then methodically dismantles the premise by noting the product hasn't shipped, isn't finished, and its key feature remains unresolved. He raises strategic questions about why Microsoft would reveal an unfinished killer feature months before launch, giving Apple time to respond. He fairly acknowledges the vaporware charge cuts both ways, noting Apple's own unshipped 'iTV' announcement.
key_points: The Zune's Wi-Fi sharing feature is being treated as a competitive threat to iPod despite the product being unfinished vaporware
Microsoft's own engineers admit the Wi-Fi power management problem isn't solved yet, undermining claims the feature is a game-changer
Strategically, announcing an unfinished killer feature early tips your hand to competitors and wastes media attention before consumers can buy
Apple's stock price was unmoved by the Zune announcement, contradicting the suggestion that investors were worried
Gruber acknowledges fairness by noting Apple's own 'iTV' announcement is equally vaporware
money_quotes: How about we wait until Microsoft decides "what they're going to do about" Zune's Wi-Fi sharing before we start scheduling dance lessons in preparation for a trip to the iPod's grave?
If they're "not done with it yet", on September 14, when will they be? How late can they wait to go into production and still hit shelves for the holiday season?
And if this really is a killer feature in a product they honestly expect to ship within the next month or two, why are they talking about it now? Why tip their hand to Apple in advance?
Why blow all this media attention before people can actually fork over their money for the thing?
The "vaporware" charge cuts both ways, of course — it would be just as unfair to ask now for Microsoft's response to "iTV".
And the most important question of all: Brown?
key_insight: Tech media's eagerness to declare unfinished vaporware a competitive threat reveals a pattern of rewarding announcements over actual shipping products.
